title: The desk at Strawberry Studio
summary: A profile of Eddie Foulkes, a Manchester sound engineer who shaped the mix of a now-famous 1994 indie record at Strawberry Studio in Stockport. He has stayed unrecognised for three decades. A young researcher tracks him down to a small workshop in Levenshulme.
body:
It is not the case that nobody knew. The producer knew, the drummer knew, the woman who ran the front desk knew. What had happened, over the thirty years since the record came out, was that the credit-line had quietly contracted. By 2024 only one name appeared above the title on streaming platforms, and that name was not Eddie Foulkes.

I met him on a Wednesday afternoon at the back of a unit on the industrial estate behind Levenshulme. He repairs guitar amplifiers now. He has done so since 2011. He is sixty-eight, walks with a slight stoop, and has the dry, deflecting humour of a man who decided long ago that being right about something is not the same as being acknowledged for it.

What Eddie did at Strawberry, in the eleven weeks of mixing between February and April 1994, is hard to summarise in a way that does not either inflate or undersell it. He did not write the songs. He did not play on the record. What he did was decide, in a thousand small judgements, where the snare drum sat in relation to the vocal, how much room ambience to bleed under the choruses, when to push a guitar back to make space for a syllable. The mix makes the chorus feel like an opening, not a wall.

The drummer told me, on the phone, that nobody else in the room could hear what Eddie could hear. The producer, when I called him in Los Angeles, was more measured but said much the same: 'He was the one who solved the second verse. Eddie heard the problem in the cymbal, of all things, and we fixed it in an afternoon.' Asked why Eddie was not credited, the producer paused, and said: 'Because none of us thought, in 1994, that the record would matter in 2024. By the time it did, the credits were what they were.'

Eddie himself is harder to draw on the subject. When I asked him whether the lack of recognition bothered him, he said: 'It would bother me more if the record was bad.' He said this without bitterness, in the manner of a man closing a topic. He returned to the amplifier he was repairing — a 1968 Vox, owned now by a teacher in Didsbury.

What is interesting is not the injustice. Injustices this size are common in the industry and the literature on them is long. What is interesting is that Eddie does not seem to want it corrected. He told me, when I pressed, that being on the record under his own name would change his memory of those eleven weeks, and that he would rather keep the memory than gain the line. I do not know whether to believe him. I am not sure he knows either. But the workshop is warm, the kettle is on, and there is a queue of amplifiers behind him that he is, in his way, the only person able to fix.

grammarNotes.title: Hedged claim + cleft + 'X was what X was' for retrospective injustice
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn mở dùng cấu trúc B2 đặc trưng: 'It is not the case that nobody knew' — phủ định lịch sự một giả định ngầm. Trong câu trích của nhà sản xuất, cleft 'He was the one who solved the second verse' đẩy tiêu điểm vào Eddie, và cụm 'the credits were what they were' là dạng tautology biểu đạt sự bất khả vãn hồi. Cấu trúc cuối — 'being right about something is not the same as being acknowledged for it' — dùng danh động từ song song để phân biệt hai khái niệm gần nhau, dấu hiệu B2 trưởng thành.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening uses the characteristic B2 'It is not the case that nobody knew' — politely negating a hidden assumption. In the producer's quote, the cleft 'He was the one who solved the second verse' refocuses on Eddie, and the phrase 'the credits were what they were' is a tautology used to mark something that cannot now be undone. The structure 'being right about something is not the same as being acknowledged for it' uses parallel gerunds to separate two adjacent concepts — a mark of mature B2.

translationVi:
Không phải là không ai biết. Nhà sản xuất biết, tay trống biết, người phụ nữ trực bàn lễ tân và ký tên cho khách vào cũng biết. Điều đã xảy ra, suốt ba mươi năm kể từ khi đĩa nhạc ra mắt, là dòng ghi công đã âm thầm bị thu hẹp. Đến năm 2024, chỉ một cái tên hiện phía trên tên đĩa trên các nền tảng nghe nhạc, và tên đó không phải Eddie Foulkes.

Tôi gặp ông một chiều thứ Tư ở phía sau một căn xưởng trong khu công nghiệp sau Levenshulme. Giờ ông sửa ampli ghi-ta. Ông đã làm vậy từ năm 2011. Sáu mươi tám tuổi, đi hơi khom lưng, và có kiểu hài hước khô khan, né tránh của một người từ lâu đã quyết rằng đúng về một điều gì đó không giống với được công nhận về nó.

Điều Eddie đã làm ở Strawberry, trong mười một tuần mix giữa tháng Hai và tháng Tư năm 1994, khó tóm lược theo cách không thổi phồng cũng không xem nhẹ. Ông không viết bài. Ông không chơi nhạc cụ. Cái ông làm là quyết, trong cả ngàn phán đoán nhỏ, vị trí của trống snare so với giọng hát, lượng âm vang phòng cần để rò dưới các đoạn điệp khúc, khi nào đẩy đàn lùi lại để chừa chỗ cho một âm tiết. Phần mix là phần của một đĩa nhạc mà người nghe không có ý thức nghe thấy. Đó là cái khiến đoạn điệp khúc nghe như một sự mở ra, không phải một bức tường.

Tay trống nói với tôi, qua điện thoại, rằng không ai khác trong phòng nghe được điều Eddie nghe được. Nhà sản xuất, khi tôi gọi cho ông ở Los Angeles, thận trọng hơn nhưng nói gần như cùng ý: 'Anh ấy là người giải quyết được đoạn hai. Tôi đã bỏ cuộc với nó. Eddie nghe ra vấn đề ở cái xập-xoã, không ngờ là chính nó, và mình sửa được trong một buổi chiều.' Khi được hỏi vì sao Eddie không được ghi công, nhà sản xuất ngừng lại, rồi nói: 'Vì không ai trong chúng tôi, vào năm 1994, nghĩ rằng đĩa này sẽ còn quan trọng năm 2024. Đến khi nó quan trọng thì phần ghi công đã là cái nó là.'

Bản thân Eddie thì khó kéo vào chủ đề này hơn. Khi tôi hỏi thẳng việc không được công nhận có làm ông phiền lòng không, ông nói: 'Nó sẽ phiền tôi hơn nếu đĩa nhạc dở.' Ông nói câu đó không cay đắng, theo kiểu một người đang khép lại chủ đề. Ông quay lại ampli đang sửa — một chiếc Vox đời 1968, giờ thuộc về một giáo viên ở Didsbury — và tiếp tục vặn một con vít.

Điều thú vị, có lẽ, không phải sự bất công. Bất công cỡ này phổ biến trong ngành nhạc và tài liệu viết về chúng đã nhiều. Điều thú vị là Eddie có vẻ không muốn sự bất công đó được sửa. Khi bị tôi gặng, ông nói rằng việc đứng tên trên đĩa, theo ông, sẽ làm thay đổi ký ức của ông về mười một tuần đó, và ông thà giữ lại ký ức còn hơn được thêm dòng ghi công. Tôi không biết có nên tin ông không. Tôi cũng không chắc ông tự biết. Nhưng xưởng thì ấm, ấm nước đang đun, và có một hàng dài ampli sau lưng ông mà chỉ ông, theo cách của mình, đủ sức sửa.

questions.prompt: What does the journalist mean by 'It is not the case that nobody knew'?
options: Several people in the original room did know Eddie's role; the issue is that the credit-line later shrank.
Nobody at the time knew anything about Eddie.
questions.explanation: Opening sentence followed by the list of people who knew.

questions.prompt: What does the journalist describe as having happened to the credit-line over thirty years?
options: It had quietly contracted; by 2024 only one name appeared above the title on streaming platforms.
It had been replaced with a question mark.

questions.prompt: Where does Eddie work now, and doing what?
options: At a unit in an industrial estate behind Levenshulme, repairing guitar amplifiers since 2011.

questions.prompt: How is Eddie's personal manner described?
options: Sixty-eight, with a slight stoop, dry deflecting humour — a man who long ago decided that being right and being acknowledged are different things.

questions.prompt: Which of these did Eddie NOT do on the record?
options: Write songs or play on it.
Decide where the snare sat in relation to the vocal.
Push a guitar back to make space for a syllable.
questions.explanation: Third paragraph explicitly: 'He did not write the songs. He did not play on the record.'
questions.hint: What the third paragraph rules out.

questions.prompt: What metaphor does the journalist use for a well-mixed chorus?
options: It feels like an opening rather than a wall.
It feels like a door slamming shut.

questions.prompt: What did the drummer say on the phone?
options: Nobody else in the room could hear what Eddie could hear.
Eddie was difficult to work with.

questions.prompt: What specific problem did Eddie solve, according to the producer?
options: The second verse — by hearing the problem in the cymbal, of all things, and fixing it in an afternoon.

questions.prompt: How does the producer explain Eddie's lack of credit?
options: In 1994 nobody thought the record would matter in 2024; by the time it did, the credits were what they were.
Eddie was a session player and not eligible.

questions.prompt: What is Eddie's answer when asked directly whether the lack of recognition bothers him?
options: 'It would bother me more if the record was bad' — said without bitterness, closing the topic.
He says he is furious and plans to sue.
He says it bothers him constantly.
He refuses to answer at all.

questions.prompt: What is Eddie doing while he answers?
options: Tightening a screw on a 1968 Vox amplifier belonging to a teacher in Didsbury.

questions.prompt: What does the journalist say is 'interesting' about the case — and what does he say is NOT the interesting part?
options: The injustice is not the interesting part (such injustices are common in the music industry); what is interesting is that Eddie does not seem to want the injustice corrected.
The injustice is the only interesting part.
The producer's behaviour is the interesting part.
questions.hint: What the sixth paragraph reframes as interesting.

questions.prompt: What reason does Eddie give for not wanting recognition now?
options: Being on the record under his own name would, in his view, change his memory of those eleven weeks; he would rather keep the memory than gain the line.

questions.prompt: What is the journalist's own stance at the end?
options: He is unsure whether to believe Eddie, and is not sure Eddie knows either — but the workshop, the kettle and the queue of amplifiers speak for themselves.
He is certain Eddie is lying.
He is certain Eddie is telling the whole truth.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the passage NOT claiming?
options: That Eddie's lack of credit is rare in the music industry.
That mix work shapes how listeners feel a chorus without their conscious awareness.
That at least three people in the original room knew Eddie's contribution.
That Eddie's stated reason for declining recognition may or may not be his real reason.
questions.explanation: The passage says such injustices are common, with a 'long literature'.
questions.hint: Look for the option contradicted by 'common in the music industry'.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the piece?
options: Careful, hedged, and observant — sympathetic without pleading the case.
questions.explanation: Note hedges like 'perhaps', 'I do not know whether to believe him'.

options: Some craft contributions are decisive yet uncreditable, and the person who made them may have legitimate reasons — even if not transparent to himself — to leave the credit alone.

cloze.template:

I met him on a Wednesday afternoon at the back of a unit on the industrial estate behind {{b3}}. He repairs guitar {{b4}} now. He has done so since 2011. What he did was decide, in a thousand small judgements, where the {{b6}} drum sat in relation to the vocal, how much room {{b7}} to bleed under the choruses. The mix is what makes the chorus feel like an opening rather than a {{b8}}.

The drummer told me, on the phone, that nobody else in the room could hear what Eddie could hear. The producer said: 'He was the one who solved the second {{b9}}. Eddie heard the problem in the {{b10}}, of all things.' Asked why Eddie was not credited, he said: 'By the time it did matter, the credits were what they {{b11}}.'

Eddie himself is harder to draw. When I asked him whether the lack of recognition bothered him, he said: 'It would bother me more if the record was {{b12}}.' He returned to the amplifier he was repairing — a 1968 Vox, owned by a teacher in {{b13}}.

What is interesting is not the injustice. What is interesting is that Eddie does not seem to want the injustice {{b14}}. He told me, when I pressed, that being on the record under his own name would change his memory of those eleven weeks, and that he would rather keep the {{b15}} than gain the line.

blanks.explanation: Does not want the injustice corrected.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Eddie tells the journalist that having his name added to the record now would change his memory of those eleven weeks at Strawberry — and that the memory is worth more to him than the line. The journalist isn't sure he believes it, and isn't sure Eddie does either. If you were the journalist, would you press further, or leave the explanation as Eddie gave it? What does each choice say about the kind of recognition we owe people whose work has been quietly absorbed by other names?
